content.full_text:
P78-02-637

February 17, 1978


Mr. Fred M. Whitaker, Treasurer
41st Assembly District Republican Central
Committee
136 South Brand Boulevard
Glendale, California 91204

Dear Mr. Whitaker:

This letter is written to confirm the advice provided to you
during our telephone conversation of February 15, 1978. My
advice was in response to your letters of January 31, 1978,
and September 20, 1077, in which you asked if the 41st Assembly
District Republican Central Committee is required to file campaign
disclosure statements.

My advice was that your committee is required to file campaign
disclosure statements because it is an official committee of
a political party and it receives contributions of $500 or more
during a calendar year. This advice is based on the definition
of "committee" contained in Government Code Section 82013(a)
and clarifications of the terms "contribution" and "expenditure"
found in 2 California Administrative Code Sections 18215 and 18225
(enclosed).

Enclosed please find a copy of the Political Reform Act and
technical assistance materials regarding the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Act. Note especially the discussion of semi-
annual statements and abbreviated statements found in the
"Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the
Political Reform Act."

Mr. Fred M. Whitaker                -2-                February 17, 1978


Also, please be advised that I contacted the Office of the
Secretary of State and they confirmed that your committee
has filed a Statement of Organization (dated August 16, 1977)
and your committee has received identification number 771267.

Please contact me if I can be of further assistance.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        JOHN GREENWOOD
                                        Special Compliance Representative


Enclosures

Republican Party

of the 41st Assembly District
Burbank-Glendale-Tujunga

136 South Brand Boulevard
Glendale, California 91204
Telephone 242-3146

Members of the Central Committee

J. Paul Vidas, Chairman
Louis W. Barnett
Allen E. Brandstater
Gerald S. Campbell
Paul Downing
Elva Kegan
Fred M. Whitaker
Marie A. Schumacher
Precinct Chairman

January 31, 1978

Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box # 807
Sacramento, California

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

On September 20, 1977, I wrote asking for a legal opinion and,
to the best of my knowledge, have thus far, received no answer.

The question that concerns us is the reporting requirements of the
Political Reform Act. We do not feel this applies to our particular
activity; however, your legal opinion would clarify the issue.

A copy of my September 20, 1977 letter is attached.

May we hear from you as soon as possible.

Sincerely,

FRED M. WHITAKER TREASURER
41st ASSEMBLY DISTRICT REPUBLICAN
CENTRAL COMMITTEE

FMW/gmv
Enc. 1

Republican Party

of the 41st Assembly District
Burbank-Glendale-Tujunga

136 South Brand Boulevard
Glendale, California 91204
Telephone 242-3146

Members of the Central Committee

J. Paul Vidas, Chairman
Louis W. Barnett
Allen E. Brandstater
Gerald S. Campbell
Paul Downing
Elva Regan
Fred M. Whitaker
Marie A. Schumacher
Precinct Chairman

September 20, 1977


Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

Marge Nunes of the Political Reform Division, Office of the Secretary of
State, 111 Capitol Mall, Room 200, Sacramento, California has suggested
we write to you for a legal opinion.

We question whether we fall under the reporting requirements of the
Political Reform Act. Apparently, the purpose of the Act is to require
committees who raise monies, make contributions, pay expenses, make
pledges or give loans to political candidates to have an ID# and to file
reports as to who and in what amounts such monies were paid.

Our organization's purpose is to raise money for the year-round maintenance
of a volunteer headquarters whose function is to get out the vote and
increase voter registration. We are not controlled by any candidate nor
do we give any monies to political candidates. The monies we raise pay
for headquarters rental, telephone, postage and other pure maintenance
functions.

In this matter, Marge Nunes felt we may not have to file; however, she
suggested we write directly to you for your legal opinion.

It is our desire to comply fully with the law, at the same time, as a
volunteer citizen organization we would appreciate being free of unnecessary
reporting requirements.

Sincerely,

Fred M. Whitaker, Treasurer
41st Assembly District Republican Central Committee
and Republican Buck and Ballot Brigade
ID# 771267

P78-02237

February 17, 1978


Mr. Fred M. Whitaker, Treasurer
41st Assembly District Republican Central
Committee
136 South Brand Boulevard
Glendale, California 91204

Dear Mr. Whitaker:

This letter is written to confirm the advice provided to you
during our telephone conversation of February 15, 1978. My
advice was in response to your letters of January 31, 1978,
and September 20, 1977, in which you asked if the 41st Assembly
District Republican Central Committee is required to file campaign
disclosure statements.

My advice was that your committee is required to file campaign
disclosure statements because it is an official committee of
a political party and it receives contributions of $500 or more
during a calendar year. This advice is based on the definition
of "committee" contained in Government Code Section 82013(a)
and clarifications of the terms "contribution" and "expenditure"
found in 2 California Administrative Code Sections 18215 and 18225
(enclosed).

Enclosed please find a copy of the Political Reform Act and
technical assistance materials regarding the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Act. Note especially the discussion of semi-
annual statements and abbreviated statements found in the
"Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the
Political Reform Act."

Mr. Fred M. Whitaker                    -2-                    February 17, 1978


Also, please be advised that I contacted the Office of the
Secretary of State and they confirmed that your committee
has filed a Statement of Organization (dated August 16, 1977)
and your committee has received identification number 771267.

Please contact me if I can be of further assistance.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        JOHN GREENWOOD
                                        Special Compliance Representative


Enclosures

sections.question: We question whether we fall under the reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act. Apparently, the purpose of the Act is to require committees who raise monies, make contributions, pay expenses, make pledges or give loans to political candidates to have an ID# and to file reports as to who and in what amounts such monies were paid. Our organization's purpose is to raise money for the year-round maintenance of a volunteer headquarters whose function is to get out the vote and increase voter registration. We are not controlled by any candidate nor do we give any monies to political candidates.
sections.conclusion: This advice is based on the 'committee' contained in Government Code Section clarifications of the 'contribution' and in 2 California Administrative Code Sections (enclosed). Our advice is that your committee is required to file campaign statements because it is an official committee of a political party and it receives contributions of $500 during a calendar year.
sections.question_synthetic: Must the 41st Assembly District Republican Central Committee file campaign disclosure statements under the Political Reform Act if it receives contributions of $500 or more during a calendar year, given that its primary purpose is to maintain a volunteer headquarters for voter registration and turnout activities rather than directly supporting candidates?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The 41st Assembly District Republican Central Committee is required to file campaign disclosure statements because it qualifies as an official committee of a political party that receives contributions of $500 or more during a calendar year, regardless of whether it directly supports candidates.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a compiled correspondence file including incoming request (Sep 20, 1977), follow-up letter (Jan 31, 1978), and FPPC response (Feb 17, 1978). Quality of document image varies; some text is degraded or unclear. The verbatim conclusion text appears incomplete in the document scan. The document clearly meets criteria as an FPPC advice letter responding to the committee's question about reporting requirements.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: We question whether we fall under the reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act. Apparently, the purpose of the Act is to require committees who raise monies, make contributions, pay expenses, make pledges or give loans to political candidates to have an ID# and to file reports as to who and in what amounts such monies were paid. Our organization's purpose is to raise money for the year-round maintenance of a volunteer headquarters whose function is to get out the vote and increase voter registration. We are not controlled by any candidate nor do we give any monies to political candidates.

CONCLUSION: This advice is based on the 'committee' contained in Government Code Section clarifications of the 'contribution' and in 2 California Administrative Code Sections (enclosed). Our advice is that your committee is required to file campaign statements because it is an official committee of a political party and it receives contributions of $500 during a calendar year.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises the 41st Assembly District Republican Central Committee that it must file campaign disclosure statements as an official party committee receiving $500+ in annual contributions. The FPPC provides reference materials and technical assistance regarding the Political Reform Act's disclosure requirements.
